name: World War I
summary: World War I was a global industrial war fought from July 1914 to November 1918 between the Allied and Associated Powers and the Central Powers. Military deaths are comparatively well documented at roughly 9.0-10.1 million, while civilian deaths are far less certain because famine, blockade, deportation, occupation, disease, and influenza are inconsistently counted. Total deaths are commonly estimated at about 15-22 million.

military_killed.notes: Range from Prost's table in 1914-1918 Online: Urlanis low and Prost evaluation high. Britannica/U.S. War Department cross-check gives 5,142,631 Allied killed and died. Definitions vary on disease, POWs, missing, and colonial laborers.

breakdown.notes: Official German/UK War Office figure for German civilians killed by Allied air raids; this is a partial category, not a full Allied direct-killing total.

breakdown.victims_group: Ottoman Muslim civilians in Eastern Anatolia
breakdown.notes: Ottoman/Middle East war-loss literature describes refugees fleeing the Russian army and Armenian bands, with deaths from hunger, disease, or massacre, but does not provide a clean aggregate for direct Allied killings.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: No reliable all-theater total for civilians directly killed by Allied forces was found. The numeric low is only a documented partial figure for Allied air raids on Germany.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Civilian excess deaths associated with food and fuel shortages under the Allied blockade of Germany. Attribution is contested because German administration, black markets, influenza, and post-armistice blockade continuation also mattered.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in Ottoman Syria and Mount Lebanon
deaths_from_actions.notes: Co-caused by Allied naval blockade, Ottoman requisitioning and administrative failure, transport disruption, and natural disasters. Not additive with the same event listed under Central Powers responsibility.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Ottoman Muslim civilians in Eastern Anatolia
deaths_from_actions.notes: Ottoman records cited by 1914-1918 Online list 701,166 Muslim refugees dying on the road; total Muslim deaths in Eastern Anatolia are estimated around 1.5 million. Deaths mix Russian invasion, Armenian bands, starvation, disease, massacre, and displacement; attribution is not clean.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: African colonial laborers and carriers serving Allied colonial forces
deaths_from_actions.notes: Minimum from British, Belgian, and Portuguese colonial laborer deaths in 1914-1918 Online; French laborer deaths are listed as unknown, so this is an undercount. Some laborers were military auxiliaries rather than civilians.

Approximate African civilian losses from war zones, rebellions, famine, malnutrition, and disease. Allied colonial powers caused much of the mobilization and requisitioning, but German and other forces also contributed; not additive with Central Powers African responsibility.

military_killed.notes: Range from Prost's table in 1914-1918 Online: Overmans low and Prost evaluation high. Britannica/U.S. War Department cross-check gives a lower 3,386,200 killed and died, less inclusive for some categories.

breakdown.notes: Horne and Kramer estimate about 6,500 Belgian and French civilians killed in German atrocities in 1914.
German massacre at Dinant on 23 August 1914; included within the broader 1914 atrocity total.
Killed during the German sack of Louvain; included within the broader 1914 atrocity total.

breakdown.victims_group: Passengers and crew of RMS Lusitania
breakdown.notes: German U-boat sinking of Lusitania; not all victims were civilians, but it was a major direct attack on a passenger liner.
Approximate deaths from German air raids and naval shelling on Britain; not a complete global bombing/shelling total.

civilians_killed_directly.notes: Direct civilian-killing totals are incomplete. Genocidal deportations and famine policies are recorded under deaths_from_actions to avoid double counting direct massacre and indirect death-march/starvation deaths.

deaths_from_actions.notes: Range combines USHMM low estimate and broader 1914-1918 Online summary. Deaths resulted from Ottoman deportation, massacre, starvation, disease, death marches, and forced labor.
Britannica states estimates vary but upwards of 250,000 Assyrians were killed during World War I.
Academic estimates for the broader 1914-1922/23 period. The WWI-only portion cannot be cleanly separated from post-armistice violence.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: Civilians in Ottoman Syria and Mount Lebanon
deaths_from_actions.notes: Co-caused by Ottoman requisitioning and administration, Allied naval blockade, transport breakdown, and natural disasters. Not additive with the same event listed under Allied responsibility.

Civilian losses from Serbian war-loss estimates, mostly typhus, famine, disease, occupation, and clashes. Caused by Central Powers invasion/occupation conditions but not all deaths were deliberate killings.
Civilian losses in Romania from 1914-1918 Online estimates; causes include disease, hunger, displacement, and occupation conditions.
Belgian civilians who died during expulsion, deportation, imprisonment, or death sentences under German occupation; separate from the approximately 6,000-6,500 murdered in 1914.

deaths_from_actions.victims_group: African soldiers and laborers in German colonial service
deaths_from_actions.notes: 1914-1918 Online table assigns about 14,000 African soldier deaths and 6,400 African laborer deaths to German colonies.

East African civilian losses from war zone, rebellion, and famine. German operations and requisitioning contributed, but Allied requisitioning and campaigning also contributed; not solely attributable to Germany and not additive with Allied African responsibility.

totals.notes: Military low/high sums Prost's Allied and Central Powers ranges. Civilian low/high uses the common global range of about 6-13 million because per-side civilian attribution is not clean and many events are co-caused. Grand total reflects the commonly cited 15-22 million WWI deaths.

key_atrocities.name: Armenian genocide
key_atrocities.notes: Ottoman deportation, death marches, massacre, starvation, disease, and forced labor. High end from broader scholarly range; USHMM gives 664,000-1.2 million.

key_atrocities.name: Eastern Anatolia Muslim refugee catastrophe
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Mixed / partly Allied Russian forces and Armenian armed groups; partly war conditions
key_atrocities.notes: Deaths from hunger, disease, massacre, and displacement while fleeing Russian invasion and Armenian bands; total attribution is contested and mixed.

key_atrocities.name: Great Famine of Mount Lebanon / Ottoman Syria
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Mixed / Ottoman authorities and Allied blockade
key_atrocities.victims_group: Civilians in Mount Lebanon and Syria
key_atrocities.notes: About 200,000 in Beirut/Mount Lebanon; about 500,000 in wider Syria. Co-caused by Allied blockade, Ottoman policy, transport disruption, and natural disasters.

key_atrocities.name: German atrocities in Belgium and northern France
key_atrocities.notes: Scholarly estimate by Horne and Kramer; includes multiple massacres during the 1914 invasion.

key_atrocities.name: Sack of Dinant
key_atrocities.notes: Massacre at Dinant on 23 August 1914; included within broader German atrocities total.

key_atrocities.name: Allied blockade of Germany
key_atrocities.notes: Excess civilian deaths associated with malnutrition and disease under blockade; exact causal attribution remains disputed.

key_atrocities.name: African campaigns and porterage deaths
key_atrocities.perpetrator: Mixed / Allied and Central colonial forces
key_atrocities.victims_group: African soldiers, laborers, carriers, and civilians
key_atrocities.notes: Approximately 255,000 African soldiers/laborers and 742,500 African civilians died; caused by colonial mobilization, porterage, requisition, famine, disease, and war-zone devastation by multiple European powers.

uncertainty_notes: Military losses are comparatively well documented but vary depending on treatment of POWs, disease, missing, post-discharge deaths, colonial troops, and laborers. Civilian losses are much less certain: no belligerent kept a complete civilian war-death registry, many estimates include or exclude the 1918-1919 influenza pandemic differently, and major mortality events such as the Allied blockade, Syrian famine, East African requisition/famine, and Eastern Anatolia refugee catastrophe had multiple interacting causes. Per-side deaths_from_actions entries should not be mechanically summed because several events are deliberately listed under more than one side as shared or non-partitionable responsibility.
